steps.title: Find the key contrast
steps.content:
The sentence presents two independent clauses: “Okinaka doesn’t make such decisions single-handedly” and “historical designations must be approved by a group of nine other experts.” The word “however” signals a contrast between them.

We need punctuation that properly joins two independent clauses with this transition word.

steps.title: Apply the rule for conjunctive adverbs
steps.content:
The original text already has a comma after “single-handedly.” Choice A — however. All places “however” after that comma, then ends with a period: IC, however. IC. Here “however” functions as a trailing conjunctive adverb closing the first independent clause, set off by the pre-existing comma; the period then ends that sentence, and “All” (capitalized) begins a new independent sentence. This is grammatically correct.

  • Choice B — however all: Inserts “however” with no surrounding punctuation, creating a run-on. Eliminated.
  • Choice C — however and all: Adds a coordinating conjunction without restructuring the clauses properly, producing an illogical construction. Eliminated.
  • Choice D — however, all: Places a comma after “however” with lowercase “all,” making both clauses part of one sentence — a comma splice between two independent clauses. Eliminated.
That matches Choice A — however. All — A.
